summary: High‑energy set with scorching Jack Straw and soaring Morning Dew
blurb: The show erupts from the opening Jack Straw into a tight New Speedway Boogie, then builds through Watchtower into an emotional Morning Dew. Jerry’s guitar is on fire while Vince adds confident keyboards; vocal flubs and a few rough spots keep it from perfection.
review: Recorded on March 23, 1991 at the Knickerbocker Arena, this Albany concert crackles with raw intensity. The first set launches with a scorching "Jack Straw" that sets an urgent tone, followed by a driving "New Speedway Boogie" and a bright "Bertha." The second set never lets up—"Playing In The Band" segues into a thunderous drum duet, then into "Space" and a blistering "All Along the Watchtower" that leads straight into one of the night’s most talked‑about moments: an emotive, soaring "Morning Dew." Reviewers repeatedly note Jerry Garcia’s powerful tone and shredding solos on both Watchtower and Dew, while Vince Welnick’s growing confidence shines through his solo work and a haunting keyboard intro on the encore. Vocal flubs and occasional rough chords (notably in "Saint Of Circumstance") keep the performance from being flawless, but the overall momentum and high‑octane jams make this show a must‑listen for fans of early‑'90s Dead energy. The clear soundboard capture (gd1991-03-23.sbd.miller.87729.sbeok.flac16) offers the best listening experience, preserving the band’s urgency without the muddiness found in many audience tapes.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: Multiple recordings highlight the same standout moments—Jack Straw, Watchtower, and Morning Dew—with enthusiastic language ('shredding', 'soaring'). Although several reviews mention vocal flubs and occasional instrumental slips, the overall tone is moderately positive, justifying a mid‑range sentiment boost.

best_recording.reason: Clear soundboard source with good fidelity; captures the high‑energy performance and all standout songs without dropouts.

key_highlights: High‑octane first set featuring "Jack Straw" and a tight "New Speedway Boogie".
"All Along The Watchtower" into an emotionally charged "Morning Dew" that many call a highlight of the era.
Vince Welnick’s confident keyboard work, especially the haunting encore intro.
Despite vocal flubs, the band maintains relentless momentum through both sets.

band_performance.Jerry: Delivers powerful, urgent guitar tones; shredding on Watchtower and delivering an emotive solo on Morning Dew.
band_performance.Phil: Provides a solid low‑end foundation; bass work during Space is highlighted as physically shaking listeners.
band_performance.Bob: Shows a few rough chord moments during "Saint Of Circumstance" but otherwise stays in the groove.
band_performance.Brent: Vince Welnick shows growing confidence, contributes effective solo work and a haunting keyboard intro on the encore; his playing helps fill the gap left by Bruce Hornsby's absence.
